coredata.dc:title: Translocation mechanism of anticancer drugs through membrane with the assistance of graphene quantum dot
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Weng, Luxi
Ren, Hao
Xu, Ruru
Xu, Jiahao
Lin, Jun
Shen, Jia-Wei
Zheng, Yongke
coredata.dc:description: In recent years, as a new type of quasi-zero-dimensional nanomaterials, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have shown excellent performance in advanced drug targeted delivery and controlled release. In this work, the delivery process of model drugs translocating into POPC lipid membrane with the assistance of GQDs was investigated via molecular dynamics (MD) simulation. Our simulation results demonstrated that a single doxorubicin (DOX) or deoxyadenine (DA) molecule is difficult to penetrate into the cell membrane. GQD7 could form sandwich-like structure with DOX and assist DOX to enter into the POPC membrane. However, due to the weak interaction with DA, both GQD7 and GQD19 can not assist DA translocating the POPC membrane in the limited MD simulation time. The drug delivery process for DOX could be divided into two steps: 1. GQDs and DOX aggregated into a cluster; 2. the aggregates enter into the POPC membrane. In all our simulation systems, if GQDs loaded with model drugs and entered the cell membrane, it had little effect on the cell membrane structure, and the cell membrane could maintain high integrity and stability. These results may promote the molecular design and application of GQD-based drug delivery systems.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Anticancer drugs
Drug delivery
Graphene quantum dot
Molecular dynamics simulation
Membrane

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Interaction of drugs with POPC membrane
Translocation of drugs into the membrane with GQDs
Translocation of DA through POPC membrane with GQD
Effect of GQDs on the structure of cell membrane after penetration of model drugs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) The structures of GQDs with different sizes; (b) the structures of model drugs DA and DOX; (c) chemical structures of POPC. (d) The snapshot of initial positon of GQDs and drug above the cell membranes at the beginning of MD simulation (take GQD7 and DOX as an example) (Cyan: VDW model for GQD7; red: licorice model for DOX; POPC in line model at the bottom of the snapshot). Water molecules were not shown for clarity.

(a) The change of the coordinates of the COM of DA in the z direction with the simulation time; (b) the instantaneous conformation diagram of the interaction between DA and cell membrane at different simulation time; (c) The coordinate of the COM of DOX in the z direction; (d) the instantaneous conformational diagram of DOX on the cell membrane at different simulation times.
instantaneous conformation of the GQD7 and DOX entering the cell membrane: (a) 3:1 system; (b) 9:1 system; and (c) 9:3 system.
The number of GQD7 clusters as a function of simulation time in different simulation systems: (a) 3:1 system; (b) 9:1 system; (c) 9:3 system.
Variation of atomic contact number between GQD7 and DOX, POPC and DOX with simulation time in different systems. (a) 3:1 system; (b) 9:1 system; (c) 9:3 system.
Van der Waals interaction and electrostatic interaction of GQD7-DOX and POPC-DOX in different systems (a) 3:1 system; (b) 9:1 system; (c) 9:3 system.
The details of all simulation systems.

head.ce:title: Translocation mechanism of anticancer drugs through membrane with the assistance of graphene quantum dot

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Rehabilitation, Affiliated Hangzhou First People’s Hospital, School of Medicine, Westlake University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In recent years, as a new type of quasi-zero-dimensional nanomaterials, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have shown excellent performance in advanced drug targeted delivery and controlled release. In this work, the delivery process of model drugs translocating into POPC lipid membrane with the assistance of GQDs was investigated via molecular dynamics (MD) simulation. Our simulation results demonstrated that a single doxorubicin (DOX) or deoxyadenine (DA) molecule is difficult to penetrate into the cell membrane. GQD7 could form sandwich-like structure with DOX and assist DOX to enter into the POPC membrane. However, due to the weak interaction with DA, both GQD7 and GQD19 can not assist DA translocating the POPC membrane in the limited MD simulation time. The drug delivery process for DOX could be divided into two steps: 1. GQDs and DOX aggregated into a cluster; 2. the aggregates enter into the POPC membrane. In all our simulation systems, if GQDs loaded with model drugs and entered the cell membrane, it had little effect on the cell membrane structure, and the cell membrane could maintain high integrity and stability. These results may promote the molecular design and application of GQD-based drug delivery systems.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: As one of the most effective means for the treatment of cancer, chemotherapy is a systemic treatment through the use of chemotherapy drugs to prevent the growth, proliferation and metastasis of tumor cells, until the tumor cells are eliminated. However, this whole-body cell-oriented treatment method also has many defects in clinical application, such as easy to produce drug resistance [1], non-specific distribution of drugs and toxic side effects on normal cells while acting on cancer cells. This leads to adverse reactions of drugs in the human body, and even damage to normal tissues and organs.

In recent years, targeted drug delivery system (TDDS) received widespread attention, which is used to control the distribution of the drug in the body, improving the effect of treatment and reduce the adverse reaction [2–5]. So far, one of the major challenges in drug delivery systems is designing suitable transport vehicles. Many new vectors such as dendritic macromolecules, polymer particles, liposomes and nanoparticles [6–11] have shown better safety and enhanced antitumor efficacy compared to free drugs. Especially, a two-dimensional carbon-based nanomaterial, graphene and its derivatives have attracted extensive attention due to its unique physical and chemical properties [12–15]. They have been widely used in gene delivery, drug delivery, interventional diagnosis and treatment, biological imaging and immunoanalysis etc [16–20]. They have shown improved drug solubility, prolong half-life, and reduced side effects.

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) can be described as a kind of zero dimensional material with characteristics derived from both graphene and carbon dots (CDs). As the materials used to synthesize GQDs, graphene and graphene oxide (GO) are composed of a single hexagonal array of sp2 hybrid carbon atoms, it contain point defects and oxygen-containing functional groups throughout the lattice and edges [21]. GQDs are carbon based nanoscale particles, making them has wide application in nanomedicine [22].

While the toxicity has been reported in many carbon-based nanoparticles due to the aggregation, GQDs offer a promising solution due to their excellent solubility in water and composition entirely of carbon, eliminating the toxicity concerns associated with metallic residues that are not cleared from the body [23]. Biocompatibility can be assessed through various methods, including cytotoxicity evaluations that measure the toxicity of an agent to cells in culture. Several studies have demonstrated the non-cytotoxicity of GQDs, with variations in cytotoxicity observed based on surface modifications [24]. Overall, GQDs have significant potential as a nontoxic alternative to metal-based nanoparticles in various biomedical applications, thanks to their high biocompatibility, low toxicity, and excellent solubility in water.

GQD-based drug delivery systems have attracted considerable attention in the field of medicine, owing to their minimal toxicity, presence of functional groups, high drug loading capacity, and ability for simultaneous tracking through photoluminescence [25]. Consequently, many studies have explored GQDs as effective delivery systems for chemotherapeutic agents such as doxorubicin (DOX) and gene therapy drug in disease treatment [26,27]. While these drugs are potent, their delivery to targeted tissues in the body is hindered by poor solubility when administered in their free forms. However, the molecular structure of DOX permits their attachment onto the basal plane of GQDs via π-π stacking interaction, which allows for delivery in a hydrophilic carrier [28]. Moreover, Feng et al. demonstrated the critical role of lipid bilayer surface charge in controlling graphene oxide attachment and release [29]. The large surface area-to-volume ratio of GQDs enables high drug loading, making them a promising alternative to traditional drug delivery systems.

Although a lot of studies have shown the advantage of GQD as a drug carrier, the translocation process of GQD carrying drug through cell membrane has not been widely investigated. The mechanism of GQDs crossing the cell membrane with drugs, as well as the thermodynamics and dynamics during the transmembrane process, have not been well understood. The effect of concentration of GQDs and type of drugs in the transmembrane process are still obscure, which limited the application of GQD as drug delivery material. In this study, DOX and DA were used as model drugs to explore the loading capability of GQD and translocation mechanism of them through membrane with the assistance of GQD at the molecular level via molecular dynamics (MD) simulation. In addition to experiments, MD simulation is a widely used method in the studies of GQD-membrane interaction [30] and transmembrane process [31–33]. For example, Song et al. illuminated the bio-interface doctrine involving the cellular translocation of Janus-shaped oxidized GQDs by molecular dynamics and well-tempered metadynamics simulations [30]. As one of the methods in computer simulation, this technique can not only obtain the trajectory of atomic motion, but also microscopic structural and dynamical details of all atoms and molecules. Thus it is a powerful tool and plays an important role in the research of drug delivery systems [34–36].

GQD7 and GQD19 (according to the number of six-membered carbon rings) were selected as the model of GQDs in this work. The structure of 1-palmitoyl-2-oleoyl-phosphatidylcholine (POPC) and model drugs (DOX and DA) in the MD simulation were derived from our previous work [37,38], as shown in Fig. 1. The POPC membrane used in the simulation consists of 274 molecules in the upper and lower layers. For all simulated systems, the initial positions of GQDS and DOX/DA were placed at 3.0 nm and 4.0 nm above the cell membrane of POPC in z direction, as shown in Fig. 1(d).

All MD simulations were carried out by GROMACS 5.0.7 [39] package and based on the CHARMM36 force field. The details of all simulation systems were shown in Table 1. The size of water box for each simulation system is around 10.3 × 10.2 × 13.0 nm3, and about 3.4 × 104 water molecules with TIP3P [40] model were used. In all MD simulations, LINCS algorithm is used to constrain all bond lengths. The time step of the simulation is 2 fs, and the cutoff of van der Waals interaction is 1.2 nm. The long

range electrostatic interaction was calculated by particle mesh Ewald (PME) summation, with a cutoff of 1.2 nm for the separation of the direct and reciprocal space summation. All systems were experienced energy minimization. After that, the temperature of all systems was kept at 310 K by Nose-Hoover thermostat, and the pre-equilibrium under the NVT ensemble of 2 ns was carried out. In this pre-equilibrium process, the position restrains of 1000 kJ⸱mol−1‧nm−2 was used to restrict the spatial position of GQDs and drugs. Then the MD simulation under the NPT ensemble of 200 ns was carried out with constant temperature of 310 K imposed by Berensdsen thermostat and 1 bar pressure controlled by a semi-isotropic Parrinello-Rahman barostat. Three-dimensional periodic boundary conditions were used in the whole simulation process. In all system simulations, the motion trajectories of MD were visualized by visual molecular dynamics (VMD) software [41].
The number of GQDs clusters was calculated to characterize the aggregation and dispersion of GQDs on both sides of cell membrane.

The interaction energy, which includes the electrostatic interaction energy (E coul) and the van der Waals interaction energy (E vdW), is used to describe the strength of the interaction between two components in a system, as shown in the formula (1)-(3):

represents the total electrostatic energy of A and B,

represents the electrostatic energy of substance A and substance B, respectively.

represents the total van der Waals (vdW) energy of A and B substances.
E

A

E
vdW

represents the van der Waals (vdW) energy of substance A and substance B, respectively.

The density of phosphorus atoms (P) in the z direction on the POPC membrane was analyzed. The thickness of POPC membrane can be estimated by calculating the distance between two peaks of P atom density distribution.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Interaction of drugs with POPC membrane
ce:section.ce:para: To understand the role of GQDs as a carrier in assisting drug penetration, the dynamics of single drug molecule (without GQDs) penetrating into cell membrane (see DA, DOX in Table 1) was explored. As shown in Fig. 2, the coordinates of the center of mass (COM) of N and P atoms in the membrane of the POPC and the drug COM in z direction (perpendicular to the membrane plane) were calculated. From the data of the z coordinates of the COM of DOX, it was found that they could not penetrate into the POPC membrane during the simulated time of 200 ns. DOX diffused in the aqueous solution or absorbed on the POPC membrane during the simulation time of 200 ns. The DOX molecule is hydrophobic, and the interaction between DOX and the hydrophilic head of POPC molecule is weak. It is similar for the interaction of POPC with DA molecule. DA was also difficult to penetrate directly into the POPC membrane within 200 ns.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Translocation of drugs into the membrane with GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: Simulation studies have shown that relatively small GQDs can penetrate into lipid membranes more easily on a nanosecond time scale [42]. Therefore, in order to study the role and mechanism of GQDs in drug delivery, we simulate the process of GQDs assisted drugs transportation across the cell membrane with different sizes, concentrations and GQD/drug ratios. As shown in Fig. 3(a), in GQD7 3:1 system, three GQD7 molecules and one DOX molecule were placed on top of the cell membrane. In the initial state, all the molecules were separated with a certain distance. Only after 5 ns, three GQD7 molecules surround the DOX molecule and form GQD7-DOX aggregates in the aqueous solution. At about 100 ns, three aggregated GQD7 molecules completely wrapped the DOX and penetrated into the POPC membrane. It implicated that the DOX could penetrate into the POPC membrane with assistance of three GQD7 molecules. After penetration of GQD7-DOX aggregate into the POPC membrane, one GQD7 molecule gradually dispersed in the cell membrane until the end of the simulation.

The phenomenon that GQD7 aggregate can disperse in the cell membrane is consistent with the findings of our previous study[43]. This is mainly due to the strong hydrophobicity of GQD7 and its strong hydrophobic interaction with the long phospholipid hydrophobic alkyl chain in the cell membrane. As seen in Fig. 3(a), other two GQD7 molecules could tightly interact with DOX molecule and form sandwich-like structure in the POPC membrane until the end of simulation. The planar structure of DOX could tightly interact with two planar GQD7 molecules through the π-π interaction. To explore the effect of GQDs concentration on the drug delivery, the system with 9 GQD7 molecules and one DOX molecule was performed. As shown in Fig. 3(b), we also observed the process of GQD7 assisted DOX transportation across the cell membrane in GQD7 9:1 system. At around 50 ns, three agglomerated GQD7 molecules encapsulated DOX and gradually embedded into the cell membrane. At 100 ns, DOX had penetrated into the cell membrane with GQD7 aggregates, and GQD7 was gradually dispersed at the end of 200 ns simulation. In GQD7 9:3 system, as seen in Fig. 3(c) and Fig. 4(c), six GQD7 molecules gather into large aggregates and wrap two DOX molecules, while another GQD7 molecule carries a single DOX molecule. At about 25 ns, three GQD7 molecules were intermingled with two DOX, forming a sandwich-like nanostructure. The DOX gradually permeated into the cell membrane with the sandwich-like structure. At 40 ns, the DOX with sandwich-like structure has completely immersed in the POPC membrane. However, the DOX with only one GQD7 molecule was still diffuse in the outside of POPC membrane until 70 ns. After that, the GQD7 with DOX molecule absorbed on the POPC membrane and gradually entered into the POPC membrane. At the end of the simulation, all DOX molecules were completely penetrated into the cell membrane and most of the GQD7 molecules also changed from the aggregated state to the dispersed state in the membrane.

The changes of the COM of GQD7, the COM of each DOX molecule and the coordinate of the COM of N atom and P atom of POPC membrane in the z direction were measured. As shown in Fig. S1 (see Supporting Information). For GQD7 3:1 system, three GQD7 could wrap the DOX at about 8 ns. The DOX entered into the cell membrane at about 22 ns in GQD7 3:1 and 18 ns in GQD7 9:1 system, respectively. In GQD7 9:3 system, three GQD7 molecules can load two DOX molecules to contact with the membrane at about 15 ns. Two DOX molecules with sandwich-like structure are brought into the cell membrane around 50 ns. The DOX molecule with only one GQD7 was gradually absorbed to the surface of the cell membrane around 165 ns, and DOX was successfully penetrated into the membrane at about 180 ns.

In the experiment, Wang et al. [44] also proved that unmodified GQDs can form a GQDs-DOX complex with DOX to effectively transport DOX to target cells. Compared with the low concentration GQD7 system, the GQD7 molecules in GQD7 9:1 system and 9:3 system could not be completely dispersed in the cell membrane during the simulation time of 200 ns, which may be due to the increase of concentration and the increase of aggregate structure. Therefore, the dynamic process of GQD7 detachment from aggregates slowed down, and the detachment process could not be completely observed in limited 200 ns MD simulations.

In order to better understand the dynamic behavior of drug delivery by GQD7, the number of GQD7 clusters versus the simulation time was calculated, as shown in Fig. 4. Because the distance between these GQD7 molecules is relatively small, its initial number of clusters is 1 in GQD7 3:1 system. After two GQD7 molecules and one DOX molecule forming sandwich-like structure, the number of GQD7 cluster is 2. With the aggregates entering into the membrane, the number of GQD7 clusters becomes 3. It means that all GQD7 molecules have been completely dispersed in the cell membrane, which can be verified by the instantaneous structure diagram in Fig. 3(a). In the 9:1 system shown in Fig. 4(b), the number of GQD7 cluster increased into 9 at around 60 ns. It also implicated that the GQD7 aggregates dispersed gradually after carrying the drug into the membrane. In 9:3 system, the number of GQD7 clusters fluctuate around 4–6 in the final 50 ns. With the increase of DOX molecules, the GQD7 could interact with more DOX molecules.

The atomic contact number and interaction energy between GQD7 and DOX, POPC and DOX were calculated to explore the interaction mechanism of GQD7 assisted DOX translocation across the cell membrane, as shown in Fig. 5 and Fig. 6. Combined with the characterization of atomic contact number and interaction energy, one can find that when GQD7 and DOX enter the cell membrane in different systems, their atomic contact number has experienced three stages: rising stage, platform stage and falling stage. With the progress of the simulation, the number of atomic contact between GQD7 and DOX gradually increase, and the van der Waals interaction energy between them also increase accordingly (rising period). With further complete contact between GQD7 and DOX, the atomic contact number reaches the maximum, and the van der Waals interaction energy between them reaches the maximum and remains stable for a period of time (platform). Subsequently, when the GQD7-DOX complex

entered the cell membrane, due to the gradual dispersion of GQD7 molecules in the GQD7 aggregate, the number of GQD7 molecules closely bound to DOX decreased gradually, and the van der Waals interaction energy between the two decreased gradually (decline period). For POPC and DOX, the number of atomic contacts between POPC and DOX increased gradually with the increase of simulation time. This is because the total number of contacts between DOX and hydrophilic head group and hydrophobic alkyl chain in cell membrane phospholipid structure kept increasing during the process of GQD7-assisted entry of DOX into cell membrane, especially in a short period of time into cell membrane, the atomic contact number and interaction energy showed a large jump.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Translocation of DA through POPC membrane with GQD
ce:section.ce:para: Firstly, we observed the process of GQD7-assisted DA entering the cell membrane and calculated the change of the number of GQD7 clusters in this process. As shown in Fig. S2(a), GQD7 molecules began to aggregate from the beginning of the simulation, and completely penetrated into the cell membrane by 50 ns, and then the number of GQD7 clusters became 3, indicating that all GQD7 molecules had been completely dispersed. In Fig. S2(b), one can also see that GQD7 is easy to form aggregates before directly interact with the cell membrane; at 30 ns, some GQD7 entered the membrane, but some GQD7 still resided outside the cell membrane in the form of large aggregates. At 150 ns, it was clear that the number of clusters of GQD7 molecules completely entering the cell membrane was 6, and GQD7 was further dispersed due to the hydrophobic interaction with hydrophobic alkyl chains. At the end of the simulation, the number of clusters was 7, indicating that most of the GQD7 molecules had dispersed in the cell membrane. When increasing the concentration of GQD7 and DA, as shown in Fig S2(c), part of GQD7 accumulated above the cell membrane during 10 ns, while part of GQD7 appears below the cell and entered the cell membrane in the form of aggregation. With the continuation of the simulation, one can find that the number of clusters has changed to 7–8. For example, the instantaneous diagram in 130 ns clearly shows that GQD7 has entered the cell membrane and most of the GQD7 molecules have been completely dispersed in the cell membrane.

We also calculated the global COM of GQD7 in different GQD7-DA systems, and the changes of the COM of each DA molecule and the coordinates of the global COM of N atom and P atom in the z direction with the simulation time. As shown in Fig. S3(a), from the change of its z-direction coordinates and the instantaneous diagram combined with its corresponding time, one can see that during 38 ns, GQD7 forms a cluster to assist DA to enter into the membrane, but around 72 ns, DA slowly move outside of the cell membrane, and is completely far away from the cell membrane at the end of the simulation. The reason may be that the interaction between GQD7 and DA is relatively small, and it is difficult for DA molecules to bind firmly to the clusters formed by GQD7 and be brought into the cell membrane by GQD7 or GQD7 aggregates. In 9:1 system (as shown in Fig. S3(b)), the GQD7 aggregates began to enter the cell membrane around 36 ns, and DA could be seen penetrating into the membrane around 180 ns (shown in the red circle), but only appeared at the top of the upper layer of the cell membrane and could not penetrate deeper into the cell membrane. For 9:3 system, as shown in Fig. S3(c), DA also failed to enter the cell membrane and dissociated outside the cell membrane throughout the simulation time. We also calculated the COM of GQD19 in different GQD19-DA systems, as well as the COM of each DA molecule and the COM coordinates of N and P atoms in the z-direction as a function of simulation time, as shown in Fig. S4. In short, it has not been observed that GQD molecules in different systems assisting DA molecules translocate across the cell membrane.

Through the above analysis and discussion, we can find that no matter GQD7 or GQD19, it is difficult to effectively carry DA into the cell membrane in the limited time of 200 ns MD simulation under several concentrations and molecular ratios we set. This may be due to the fact that DA only contains a conjugated heterocycle, and has weak π-π stacking and hydrophobic interactions with GQD7 and GQD19. In the process of entering cell membrane, both GQD7 and GQD19 can not absorb DA continuously and stably, this makes it difficult for DA to penetrate into the cell membrane with the aid of both GQDs.

It is very difficult for the GQDs-DA complex to penetrate the lipid membrane through diffusion in a limited time in all-atomic MD simulations without external forces. Therefore, it is shown in the simulation study that the application of external force on the nanoparticles is a common method to help them penetrate the lipid membrane [45].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of GQDs on the structure of cell membrane after penetration of model drugs
ce:section.ce:para: The density distribution of the P atom in the z direction of the cell membrane were measured after the penetration of GQDs and DOX, as shown in Fig. S5(a) and S5(b). In all systems, the COM of the cell membrane is set to zero in the z direction, and the cell membrane thickness can be obtained by measuring the distance between the two peaks of the P atom density. In the GQD7-DOX system, the cell membrane thicknesses of 3:1, 9:1 and 9:3 systems were 4.02 nm, 3.99 nm and 3.99 nm, respectively. In the GQD19-DOX system, the cell membrane thicknesses of 3:1, 9:1 and 9:3 systems were 4.03 nm, 4.04 nm and 4.0 nm, respectively. The cell membrane thickness of GQD7-DA system was 3.97 nm, 4.02 nm and 3.91 nm, and that of GQD19-DA system was 3.96 nm, 4.02 nm and 4.02 nm, respectively. Before the simulation, the thickness of pure cellular membrane was measured to be about 4.0 nm, which indicates that the effect of GQDs on the thickness of cellular membrane is not obvious when DOX and DA enter into cellular membrane, and the effect of GQDs on cell membrane thickness was also very small in 9:3 system (has highest concentration), which was the same as that observed in previous work of our group [46]. In recent years, some experimental studies have found that GQDs is less cytotoxic than carbon-based nanomaterials such as graphene, which may be due to its smaller size and less effect on the structure of biomacromolecules such as cell membranes [43]. Our MD simulation results also show that GQDs has little effect on the membrane structure when it is used as a nano-drug carrier, which can keep the integrity and stability of the membrane structure.

In this study, the dynamics and molecular mechanism of GQDs assisting model drugs (DOX, DA) with different sizes, concentrations and molecular ratios transport across into the cell membrane were investigated by MD simulation. With the assistance of GQDs, DOX could translocate into POPC membrane. In the translocation process, three GQD7 can form a cluster to completely wrap the DOX, and then the whole aggregated complex can be carried into the membrane; for the larger size of GQD19, because it has similar size as DOX and the interaction between GQD and DOX is relatively strong, one GQD19 molecule can help DOX enter the cell membrane. However, GQD19 is difficult to disperse in the membrane and is easy to form large aggregates. With larger size of GQD, the drug-loading capacity increase but more aggregates were found in cell membrane, as observed in our simulation. It indicates that for optimal drug delivery, one has to balance the relationship between the size of GQDs and their efficiency of drug delivery. This may be investigated by future computer simulations or experimental studies. For DA system, because of its weak interaction with GQD7, it is difficult for GQD7 to load into the cell membrane. In all our simulation systems, if GQDs loaded with model drugs and entered the cell membrane, it had little effect on the cell membrane structure, and the cell membrane could maintain high integrity and stability. Through the study of this work, we believe that GQDs as a drug carrier can better assist the transmembrane delivery of drugs with large delocalization π electrons and strong hydrophobicity. The findings and results of this work may be more helpful for understanding the molecular mechanism and dynamic properties of drug transmembrane delivery assisted by carbon-based nanomaterials such as GQDs, and provide new ideas and theoretical guidance for the design and application of GQDs-based drug delivery systems.

Luxi Weng: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Hao Ren: Writing – original draft, Validation, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Ruru Xu: Validation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Jiahao Xu: Visualization, Validation, Data curation. Jun Lin: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Conceptualization. Jia-Wei Shen: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization. Yongke Zheng: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Resources, Project administration, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant Nos. 22378090).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.colsurfb.2024.114340.
